front: Building: double-cube interior; Rubens painted the ceiling with the Apotheosis of James I
Building: only surviving part of the Palace of Whitehall, which mostly burned down in 1689; the Palace began existence as York Place
Building: James I wanted it to be part of 'the biggest palace in Christendom'; facade has Ionic and Composite pilasters with alternating segmental and triangular pediments
Building: Rubens's ceiling series includes The Union of the Crowns and ovals of virtues triumphing over vices; may have been inspired by Paolo Veronese's Triumph of Venice at the Doge's Palace
Building: first consciously classical building in Britain; originally designed for Anne of Denmark, then completed for Henrietta Maria
Building: contains the Tulip Staircase — England's first self-supporting spiral staircase
Building: the two-part design of Greenwich Hospital was split to preserve an unimpeded view from this building
Building: piazza-style development; London's first modern public square; rectangular design inspired by the Place Royale in Paris
Building: St. Paul's Church here has four Tuscan columns supporting a portico facing onto the piazza and market
Architect: credited with introducing Palladian ideals to British architecture
Architect: before turning to architecture, designed sets and costumes for Ben Jonson's court masques including The Masque of Blackness and The Hue and Cry After Cupid
Architect: pioneered use of Vitruvius's system of proportions; praised in Colen Campbell's Vitruvius Britannicus
Architect: published a reconstruction of Stonehenge claiming it was a Roman site with a hexagonal structure at its center
Architect: co-designed a garden for Frederick V of Bohemia with Salomon de Caus, described as 'the eighth wonder of the world'
Architect: in 1610s London, tried to import a theorbo (tall re-entrant bass lute with 14 courses and 2 pegboxes) but customs confiscated it
